text: The concept of 'preference falsification,' developed by economist Timur Kuran, explains how individuals publicly express opinions they privately reject to conform with perceived majority views — and how this distorts the information collective attention relies on. Everyone performs conformity; everyone privately suspects they are not alone in their dissent; no one defects because defection appears costlier than it is. When the preference cascade finally breaks — as when a single viral post reveals widespread private skepticism — the crowd can reverse direction with bewildering speed. What looked like consensus was always an equilibrium of mutual performance, held together by shared misperception of the crowd's true composition.
question: According to Kuran's theory, what holds a false preference consensus together?

options.text: Active enforcement by platform algorithms and moderators
Genuine agreement among most members of the crowd
Shared misperception of the crowd's true private composition
The high cost of producing viral content that challenges the consensus
